channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Trump Threatens Iran and Then Pulls Back, All in the Same Day
item.description: President Trump has repeatedly said he’ll restart military action against Iran, only to stop short of plunging the United States directly back into an unpopular war.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Pager and Eric Schmitt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A U.S. military official said that Iran had been able to study the flight patterns of American fighter jets and bombers.

item.title: Here’s How Iran Could Respond to Renewed U.S.-Israeli Strikes
item.description: In any new round of fighting, Iranian officials could adopt new tactics, including intensified strikes on neighbors and trying to close off a second strait.
item.dc:creator: Yeganeh Torbati
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: The Iranians have used their proximity to the Strait of Hormuz, a critical trade waterway, to exert immense pressure on the global economy.

item.title: Oil Slick Reaches a Pristine Persian Gulf Island in Iran
item.description: Videos show birds, turtles and crabs trapped inside mounds of tar around Shidvar island, a protected wildlife sanctuary with turquoise waters and white sand beaches.
item.dc:creator: Sanam Mahoozi, Erika Solomon and Devon Lum
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: A video verified by The Times shows oil coating the shoreline of Shidvar Island as smoke rises from the Lavan refinery, seen in the distance.

item.title: A Fearful Phone Call and Frantic Search Preceded San Diego Mosque Shooting
item.description: San Diego police arrived to find three people dead. Nearby, they found two teenagers, the attackers, dead in a car. The violence shattered an idyllic Southern California city.
item.dc:creator: Tim Arango, Christina Morales, Candice Reed and Chelsia Rose Marcius
item.category: Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives
item.media:credit: John Francis Peters for The New York Times
item.media:description: Police officers raced around San Diego before converging at the mosque.

item.title: What to Know About San Diego’s Islamic Center
item.description: A visitor said that the mosque is highly diverse. Its services are in English, because attendees come from all over the world.
item.dc:creator: Adeel Hassan
item.media:credit: John Francis Peters for The New York Times
item.media:description: People embracing near the scene of a shooting outside the Islamic Center of San Diego on Monday.

item.title: San Diego Mosque Shooting Comes Amid Rising Reports of Islamophobia in the U.S.
item.description: The Council on American-Islamic Relations said complaints about bias against Muslims are at levels unseen in decades.
item.dc:creator: Bernard Mokam
item.media:description: Law enforcement officers swarmed to the scene of a shooting at the Islamic Center in San Diego on Monday.

item.title: L.I.R.R. Strike Ends After Tens of Thousands Endure Painful Commutes
item.description: Commuters took lengthy detours on the third day of a strike by Long Island Rail Road workers, who had gone without raises for years. Workers and transit officials reached a deal late Monday.
item.dc:creator: Stefanos Chen and Ashley Southall
item.media:credit: Ryan Murphy for The New York Times

item.title: L-I-R-R vs. Lurr: The Debate Splitting New York Commuters
item.description: Initialism or acronym? It doesn’t matter. No one can agree on how to pronounce it.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Keh
item.media:credit: Ryan Murphy for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Long Island Rail Road is frequently abbreviated to L.I.R.R. But how is that pronounced?

item.title: Abortion Pill Lawsuit Leaves Trump in a Political Bind Ahead of the Midterms
item.description: Louisiana wants the Food and Drug Administration to curtail access to the medication. Doing so could cost Republicans at the polls.
item.dc:creator: Pam Belluck and Sheryl Gay Stolberg
item.media:description: A lawsuit against the F.D.A. threatens access to medication abortion, and the Trump administration has remained strikingly silent about the case, even as it reached the Supreme Court.

item.title: Tonys 2026 Predictions: Who Will Win? And Who Should?
item.description: Our chief theater critic looks at this year’s nominees and makes some predictions (and recommendations).
item.dc:creator: Helen Shaw
item.category: Two Strangers (Carry a Cake Across New York) (Play)
item.media:description: Susannah Flood, seated, and fellow cast members in “Liberation.”

item.title: Judge Bars ICE From Making Immigration Arrests at Courts in New York
item.description: The Trump administration’s practice of detaining migrants at federal courthouses had stirred outrage.
item.dc:creator: Jonah E. Bromwich and Benjamin Weiser
item.media:credit: Madison Swart for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mask-wearing federal agents had become a regular presence in the hallways of 26 Federal Plaza in Lower Manhattan.

item.title: Trump Moves to Admit 10,000 More White South Africans as Refugees
item.description: The Trump administration argues that an “emergency refugee situation” in South Africa merits bringing more Afrikaners to America, at a cost of some $100 million.
item.dc:creator: Zolan Kanno-Youngs and Hamed Aleaziz
item.media:description: President Trump and his aides have claimed that white South Africans face racial persecution, an assertion strongly disputed by officials in that country.

item.title: Mark Fuhrman, Flawed Witness in O.J. Simpson Trial, Dies at 74
item.description: A Los Angeles police detective, he was discredited during Mr. Simpson’s 1995 murder trial by defense lawyers who pointed to his past use of racist language.
item.dc:creator: Clyde Haberman
item.media:description: Mark Fuhrman on the witness stand during the 1995 murder trial of O.J. Simpson. He was called as a star witness for the prosecution but was thoroughly discredited by Mr. Simpson’s lawyers.

item.title: Justice Dept. Sets Up $1.8 Billion Fund That Could Funnel Money to Trump Allies
item.description: The arrangement was denounced by critics as a slush fund for supporters of President Trump, possibly including Jan. 6 rioters.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Thrush, Andrew Duehren and Alan Feuer
item.media:credit: Haiyun Jiang for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump, two of his sons and his family business had argued in the case that the Internal Revenue Service should have done more to prevent a former contractor from leaking tax information.

item.title: Top Treasury Lawyer Resigns After Creation of ‘Anti-Weaponization Fund’
item.description: Brian Morrissey, the department’s general counsel, stepped down hours after the Trump administration announced the $1.8 billion fund.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Duehren
item.media:credit: Jason Andrew for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Treasury Department’s general counsel, Brian Morrissey, resigned on Monday, three people familiar with the move said.

item.title: Hegseth Campaigns for Trump Loyalist in Kentucky House Race
item.description: The campaign appearance was an extraordinary breach of military decorum, even for Mr. Hegseth, who has stretched the boundaries of partisan politics as defense secretary.
item.dc:creator: Chris Cameron and Eric Schmitt
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Pete Hegseth, the defense secretary, testifying at the Capitol last week.

item.title: Sandy Fire in Simi Valley Threatens Thousands of Homes
item.description: Officials ordered more than 29,000 to evacuate after a brush fire broke out in Simi Valley, Calif.
item.dc:creator: Soumya Karlamangla and Amy Graff
item.media:description: The blaze, called the Sandy fire, broke out on a warm, breezy day in Simi Valley.

item.title: Channel Islands Fire Started Accidentally by Shipwrecked Sailor
item.description: The shipwrecked man was rescued from Channel Islands National Park, but the flares set off a 14,600-acre fire that has burned park buildings, forced evacuations and threatened rare Torrey pines.
item.dc:creator: Soumya Karlamangla

item.title: N.Y.C. Hotel Housekeepers Will Earn Over $100,000 Under New Contract
item.description: A deal between a powerful union and an industry group substantially boosts annual pay for workers coping with New York City’s high cost of living.
item.dc:creator: Patrick McGeehan
item.media:description: A contract agreement between hotel owners and workers averts a potential strike during the World Cup.

item.title: Pollock and Brancusi Join the $100 Million Club at Auction
item.description: A “drip” painting by the Abstract Expressionist sold for $181.2 million with fees, while a bronze head by the Romanian sculptor brought in $107.6 million at Christie’s, both from the S.I. Newhouse collection.
item.dc:creator: Julia Halperin, Tim F. Schneider and Zachary Small
item.media:description: “Number 7A, 1948,” by Jackson Pollock, one of the treasures of S.I. Newhouse’s collection at Christie’s on Monday night, sold for $181.2 million with fees.

item.title: Elon Musk Loses $150 Billion Suit Against OpenAI and Sam Altman
item.description: A nine-member jury found that Mr. Musk had waited too long to sue. The setback for the tech mogul frees OpenAI to continue in the artificial intelligence race.
item.dc:creator: Cade Metz and Mike Isaac
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times
item.media:description: OpenAI’s president, Greg Brockman, right, was also named in Mr. Musk’s lawsuit.

item.title: After Elon Musk’s Court Loss Comes the Long Hot A.I. Summer
item.description: Even as protests increase, the collapse of Mr. Musk’s suit against OpenAI and Sam Altman will speed up the artificial intelligence juggernaut.
item.dc:creator: David Streitfeld and Natallie Rocha
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times
item.media:description: Elon Musk on Monday lost his lawsuit accusing OpenAI and its chief executive, Sam Altman, of breaching the company’s founding contract.

item.title: Five Takeaways From the Blockbuster Trial Pitting Elon Musk Against OpenAI
item.description: It took a jury less than two hours to decide that Mr. Musk had waited too long to sue. But the testimony over three weeks was still illuminating.
item.dc:creator: Cade Metz
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times
item.media:description: The federal courthouse in Oakland, Calif., on Monday, when a jury decided that Elon Musk had waited too long to file his lawsuit against OpenAI.

item.title: Ebola Was Identified in Congo Weeks Before W.H.O. Declared an Emergency
item.description: Early surveillance and testing failed to identify the rare species of Ebola responsible for the current outbreak. An American doctor is among the confirmed cases.
item.dc:creator: Ruth Maclean
item.category: Africa Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
United States Agency for International Development
item.media:description: At a hospital in Ituri Province, Democratic Republic of Congo, the center of a new Ebola outbreak.

item.title: American Man Tests Positive for Ebola Amid Outbreak, Officials Say
item.description: An American doctor working with a Christian missionary organization in the Democratic Republic of Congo was exposed to the deadly virus while treating patients, the organization said.
item.dc:creator: Yan Zhuang and Hannah Ziegler
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
United States Agency for International Development
item.media:description: People wait near an ambulance at a hospital in Bunia, Congo on Sunday.

item.title: Trump’s Portrait Pops Up on Rickshaws in India to Mark America’s 250th
item.description: The president is popping up on the back of the iconic three-wheeler to kick off America’s 250th anniversary celebrations in India.
item.dc:creator: Alex Travelli
item.media:credit: Anindito Mukherjee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Md. Tayemkhan, a rickshaw driver, waited for hours near the U.S. Embassy in New Delhi to get a banner featuring President Donald Trump put on the back of his vehicle. He said he was paid about $16.

item.title: Mamdani, the ‘Tax-the-Rich’ Mayor, Reaches Out to Titans of Finance
item.description: Mayor Zohran Mamdani, who has angered some corporate executives with his tax policies, has had a recent series of meetings with top business leaders.
item.dc:creator: Dana Rubinstein and Lauren Hirsch

item.title: The Generation That Grew Up With A.I. Hates It
item.description: Why graduates are booing artificial intelligence.
item.dc:creator: Michelle Goldberg

item.title: Colorado Supreme Court Orders Hospital to Restart Care for Transgender Minors
item.description: Children’s Hospital Colorado had halted hormone therapy and puberty blockers for transgender youths amid threats from the Trump administration to withhold funding from institutions that provide it.
item.dc:creator: Jack Healy
item.media:description: The Ralph L. Carr Colorado Judicial Center, home of the Colorado Supreme Court in Denver.

item.title: South Korea and Japan’s Leaders Are Set to Meet: What to Know
item.description: Japan’s prime minister, Sanae Takaichi, and South Korea’s president, Lee Jae Myung, have surprised many observers by moving past their nations’ historical grievances.
item.dc:creator: Choe Sang-Hun
item.media:description: Japan’s prime minister, Sanae Takaichi, arriving at the Daegu International Airport in South Korea on Tuesday.

item.title: American Hantavirus Patient Ordered to Stay in Quarantine Despite Plans to Leave
item.description: A woman from the cruise ship with an outbreak of the deadly disease has been ordered to remain in an Omaha facility for two more weeks. She says she tested negative.
item.dc:creator: Sonia A. Rao
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:description: The University of Nebraska Medical Center campus in Omaha houses the National Quarantine Unit, the only federally funded facility of its kind.
